content.problemStatement:
Given a `N x N` integer matrix, find the no. of pairs of row and column which are equal.

For eg. in the following matrix:



Number of equal pairs of row and columns is: **2** (Row 2 and column 2 are equal. Similarly, row 3 and column 3 are also equal)


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer NNN, denoting the no. of rows and columns in the matrix.
Next NNN lines contains NNN space separated integers, the elements of the matrix.


Output Format

Output on a single line the no. of pairs of row and columns which are equal.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 100) denoting the size of the square matrix, followed by N lines each containing N space‑separated integers (0 ≤ a[i][j] ≤ 1000).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of pairs (i, j) such that the i‑th row and the j‑th column of the matrix are identical.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Row 2 equals column 2, Row 3 equals column 2, Row 2 equals column 3, and Row 3 equals column 3, giving four equal (row, column) pairs.
No row matches any column, so the answer is zero.
No row matches any column, so the answer is zero.

solution.approach: For each row we create a unique string representation (e.g., joining its elements with a delimiter) and store the frequency of each row pattern in a hash map. Then for each column we build the same string representation and look it up in the map; the map value tells how many rows are identical to this column. Summing these counts over all columns yields the required number of equal (row, column) pairs. The delimiter prevents ambiguous encodings (e.g., [1,12] vs [11,2]). The algorithm runs in O(N^2) time per test case and uses O(N^2) space for the matrix plus O(N) extra for the map.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) for storing the matrix; the hash map uses O(N) additional space
